steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage states P4 gave equal ratings to abstract and cubist paintings. The graph shows the model's correlation with P4 is 0.3 for abstract and 0.4 for cubist. The question asks what the model predicted given that P4's actual ratings were the same.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'P4's ratings for abstract and cubist paintings would differ from one another': If P4 actually rated both equally but the model correlated better with cubist than abstract, the model must have predicted different ratings for the two styles — its predictions didn't track the equal actuals equally well. This directly follows from unequal correlations when actual ratings are the same. ✓
  • Choice B — 'P4 would derive less aesthetic pleasure from cubist than from abstract': The higher correlation for cubist (0.4) means the model's predictions were closer to P4's actual cubist ratings, not that it predicted lower cubist enjoyment. ✗
  • Choice C — 'P4 would derive more aesthetic pleasure from cubist than from abstract': This claims the model predicted higher cubist enjoyment. But the question asks what follows from unequal correlations given equal actual ratings — not a specific direction of predicted difference. ✗
  • Choice D — 'P4's rating for abstract and cubist would equal one another': This restates the given assumption about P4's actual ratings, not what the model predicted. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
